L A BLATIONS We performed ablation to measure the impact of various self-referential components of Prompt- breeder. We investigated the following mutation operators and mechanisms: • Random initial prompts The original problem specification for the dataset is used instead of generating an initial task-prompt using the mutation prompt + thinking style + problem specification. • Random initial mutation prompts The mutation-prompt ”Please summarize and improve the following instruction:” is used instead of randomly selecting a mutation-prompt from the list. • Prompts from context (Lamarckian) 62Hyper Lamarck SR task-prompt SR mut-prompts ablation_mode ADDSUB AQUA_DEV CS_STRATEGY_QA GSM MULTIARITH SINGLEEQ STRATEGY_QA SVAMP dataset -13 -11 -23 -26 -11 -33 -58 1 -16 -15 -80 -29 -37 -36 -73 41 -62 -63 -71 9 -16 -29 -39 -34 -61 -41 -44 -57 -21 -10 -74 -41 Proportion of fitnesses above baseline (Full algorithm) -100% 0% 100% % of fitnesses above baseline Figure 4: The results of ablating the one by one the self-referential operators compared to using the full algorithm. 0% signifies an ablated operation with neither positive nor negative impact. From left to right (Hyper = Removal of mutation-prompt mutation, Lamarck = Removal of Context to task- prompt mutation, SR task-prompt = Removal of thinking-style guided task-prompt initialization, SR mut-prompt = Removal of random selection of a mutation-prompt from the mutation-prompt list.) . Percentage scores close to −100% indicate that removing the operation results in lower fitness at equivalent points in the run; conversely scores close to 100% mean that the operation is actively harmful, because individuals have higher fitnesses at equivalent points in the run when that operation is removed. The Lamarckian mutation operator that generates a task-prompt from a correct context is replaced with the default zero-/first-order prompt mutation operation (50:50 chance of one or the other) • Meta-mutation (mutating mutation-prompts) When meta-mutation would normally take place the default zero-/first-order prompt muta- tion operation is performed (50:50 chance of one or the other) For each dataset and each ablation, we use a population of 10 for 200 evaluations (equivalent to 20 generations, similar to larger experiments in this paper) and compare to the complete algorithm with the same population size and no ablations. To measure how effective an ablated operation is, we determine the proportion of evaluations in the ablation that were higher than the baseline evaluations at each generation, and sum these over all generations in the run. The results in Figure 4 show that in most cases all the mutation operators have a positive impact on fitness, with the Random Initial Prompts having the largest positive impact across all datasets. We also investigated the influence of different mutation operators on the ETHOS hate speech de- tection dataset (Mollas et al., 2022) with the under-specified problem specification "Solve the 63Problem" (in contrast to the standard problem specification "Determine whether a text contains hate speech"). Promptbreeder achieved a score of 81.6%. The greatest deteriora- tion happens when removing the Lamarckian ‘from context to prompt’ mutation method which induces the instruction from an example of the correct working out ( 64.6%). The second greatest detriment to performance happens when removing random initialization of mutation prompts, ran- dom initialization of prompts, and hyper-mutation of mutation prompts simultaneously, leaving only context mutation (68.7%). Adding back online mutation increases performance back to 70.4% and adding random mutation prompts brings this back up to 73.7%. This demonstrates the interplay and importance of Promptbreeder’s diverse set of mutation operators. 64

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Promptbreeder (PB), a self-referential, evolutionary prompt optimization system that simultaneously evolves task-prompts and the mutation-prompts that modify them. PB maintains diversity via multiple mutation operators and population-based search, overcoming diminishing returns in prior automatic prompt engineering. It achieves state-of-the-art or superior results to Chain-of-Thought, Plan-and-Solve, APE, and OPRO on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, and demonstrates domain adaptation (e.g., hate speech classification). The paper also ablates self-referential components to quantify their contributions.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: PB represents each evolutionary unit as two task-prompts plus an associated mutation-prompt (and optional few-shot contexts). Initialization composes problem description, thinking-styles, and seed mutation-prompts to generate initial task-prompts. A binary tournament genetic algorithm evaluates fitness on random batches of training Q&A (100 examples), selects winners, and applies one of nine mutation operators across five classes: (1) Direct mutation (zero-order: new prompts from problem description; first-order: mutation-prompt guided edits), (2) Estimation of Distribution (EDA) mutations using population lists with diversity filtering and a rank/index variant, plus lineage-based mutation using elite histories, (3) Hypermutation (zero-/first-order) to evolve mutation-prompts themselves, (4) Lamarckian mutation that induces new task-prompts from successful rationales/"workings out", (5) Prompt crossover and context shuffling for few-shot evolution. Diversity is maintained via BERT embedding filtering (cosine < 0.95), sampling temperature evolution for the “redescriber,” and occasional randomization. Inference uses a two-stage prompting pipeline (prompt1 -> reasoning -> prompt2 -> answer); LLM back-end primarily PaLM 2-L; Inducer/Evaluator temperature 0.0, Redescriber 1.0–2.0 with mutation.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Benchmarks: Arithmetic reasoning (GSM8K, SVAMP, MultiArith, AddSub, AQuA-RAT, SingleEq), commonsense reasoning (CommonsenseQA, StrategyQA), instruction induction (24 tasks from APE), and hate speech classification (ETHOS).
- Splits: For MultiArith, AddSub, SingleEq, SVAMP, datasets are randomly halved into train/test; other benchmarks use standard splits. Fitness evaluated on random batches of 100 training examples; final best individual evaluated on the test set.
- Algorithms compared: Manual/Zero-shot CoT, Program-of-Thoughts (PoT), Plan-and-Solve (PS, PS+), Auto-CoT, APE (zero-shot prompt), OPRO. PB uses PaLM 2-L as the underlying LLM for comparisons.
- Evolution: Population size 50, typically 20–30 generations (1–2k fitness evaluations). Two sequential task-prompts are evolved per unit. Few-shot contexts consist of previously correct “workings out.”
- Metrics: Accuracy for classification/QA; multiple-choice selection accuracy where applicable.
- Results: PB outperforms PS/PS+ and other baselines across most datasets; e.g., GSM8K zero-shot 83.9% (vs. OPRO 80.2%), and ETHOS hate speech classification 89% vs. 80% with a simple baseline prompt. PB surpasses APE on 21/24 instruction induction tasks.
- Ablations: Removing self-referential components (e.g., hypermutation, Lamarckian operator, self-referential initialization) generally harms performance; initial re-description is most beneficial.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Fixed prompting topology (two sequential prompts); PB adapts prompt content but not the prompting algorithm/graph itself.
- Relies on an externally specified fitness function over labeled data; does not invent new evaluation criteria.
- Self-referential recursion can derail or fall into attractors without diversity mechanisms; prompts can drift into low-quality/nonsensical text in some few-shot runs.
- LLM struggled to utilize explicit fitness values in EDA variants; ranking “lies” were used to promote diversity.
- Results primarily demonstrated with PaLM 2-L; generality across diverse LLMs not extensively evaluated.
- Computational cost and wall-clock efficiency are Not mentioned.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Evolve prompting topology and conditional multi-prompt “thought processes,” not only prompt content; evaluate entire reasoning procedures.
- Use LLMs to assess and promote diversity/novelty (e.g., OMNI-like interestingness measures) and to interpret fitness signals directly.
- Self-play/competitive Socratic setups to co-evolve prompts for policies or agents.
- Extend to multimodal prompting and tool-augmented reasoning; integrate open-ended, hierarchical selection mechanisms.
- Broaden evaluations across more tasks and LLM back-ends; study robustness, stability, and sample efficiency; explore resource-aware evolution and automated stopping criteria.

new_method.method:
Title: SURGE-Prompt: Surrogate-Guided, Robust, and Modular Prompt Optimization for Black-Box LLMs

Motivation
- Practical gap (プロンプト最適化の自動化): Existing black-box prompt optimizers (APE/OPRO/EvoPrompt/Promptbreeder) are token-expensive, brittle to phrasing and version drift, provide no calibrated reliability or safety guarantees, and are hard to audit. Pool-based/embedding local search (ZOPO) cannot create new high-quality prompts and lacks risk calibration; embedding/soft-prompt tuning (BBT/PEZ) needs model internals and harms interpretability/governance.
- Enterprise needs: Predictable budgets, auditability (constraint-safe, explainable edits), robustness to paraphrases and model upgrades, and measurable reliability (calibrated risk bounds on task quality and safety/format compliance).
- Scientific gap: No method jointly provides (a) an uncertainty-calibrated surrogate over modular, interpretable prompt fragments; (b) constraint-safe candidate generation with pre-call validation; (c) distributionally robust selection across paraphrases and model versions; (d) instance-adaptive reasoning toggles to trade cost/quality; and (e) budgeted reliability via conformal calibration.

Methodology
High-level approach: Learn a calibrated surrogate over a typed, modular Prompt IR (PIR) and use it to propose, rank, and verify fragment-level edits before making expensive API calls. Acquire candidates by optimizing a robust objective over paraphrases and plausible model-version shifts, with conformal guardrails on quality/safety. Provide a drop-in propose()/learn() for OPRO/EvoPrompt/ZOPO loops.

Key components (new vs prior work highlighted):
1) Typed Prompt IR (PIR) + validator (interpretable and constraint-safe)
- Fragment types: {reasoning trigger, step plan, tool/verifier hints, output schema, tone/style, safety/guardrails, brevity/cost policy}. Each has length ceilings, reserved tokens, and schema markers (e.g., JSON Schema for outputs).
- Constrained editing: A lightweight open-source LLM segments raw prompts into fragments. Edit ops = {insert/delete/swap/move/mask-infill} at fragment or span level; safety/schema fragments are immutable by default. A finite-state/CFG+JSON-Schema+regex validator rejects invalid or unsafe edits pre-API.
- Library and reuse: Maintain a fragment bank with metadata (type, semantics, historical metrics). Enables cross-task reuse, audit trails, and governance. Novelty: Prior methods do not operate over typed, validated fragments with hard constraints and audit logs.

2) Uncertainty-aware surrogate over fragmentized prompts (new)
- Inputs: (i) fragment graph with types/order; (ii) task descriptor and exemplar summary; (iii) prompt stats (length/cost/lexicality); (iv) cheap proxy scores from small OSS models on micro-batches; (v) model-ID embedding (family/version/decoding); (vi) optional input-difficulty features.
- Architecture: Fragment-graph Transformer with deep-ensemble evidential head predicting: success probability, aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty, and auxiliary risks (format violation, safety, cost overrun). Train with noise-aware losses + pairwise ranking to learn fine-grained ordering.
- Calibration: Temperature scaling + split conformal prediction yield calibrated LCB/UCB bands per candidate and per constraint. Cross-model conditioning grants rapid few-shot recalibration (<30 API calls) for unseen versions. Novelty vs ZOPO/OPRO/EvoPrompt: explicit, calibrated UQ over modular prompts rather than uncalibrated LLM scoring or fixed pools.

3) Saliency-guided, constrained span edits with causal checks (new)
- Attributions (integrated gradients/rollout) localize high-leverage spans within fragments. Mask-and-infill using a 7B OSS LLM conditioned on fragment type and PIR constraints.
- Micro-batch A/B tests: Toggle one fragment to estimate causal contribution; residuals refine the surrogate online and support interpretability. Prior work lacks causal, fragment-level edit attribution.

4) Robust multi-objective acquisition for black-box queries (new)
- Paraphrase/Version robustness: For each candidate prompt, auto-generate 3–5 paraphrases; score surrogate under a panel of model-ID embeddings that proxy plausible version shifts. Optimize robust objective: mean − α·std or CVaR across paraphrases/models, subject to hard safety/format constraints and soft cost/latency penalties.
- Batched selection: Thompson/UCB on conformal LCBs; diversify via DPP/submodular coverage over fragment embeddings to avoid mode collapse. Early stopping with Wilson LCB halts low-promise candidates after few API trials. Novel vs APE/OPRO/EvoPrompt: distributionally robust objective with calibrated bands and diversification.

5) Active learning + conformal guardrails (new)
- Prioritized replay by uncertainty/surprise; BatchBALD-like selection to probe most informative regions.
- Conformal caps on safety/format error rates at user-specified α under fixed budgets; exploration continues only within risk budget. Prior methods offer no statistical guarantees.

6) Instance-adaptive fragment router (optional; new)
- A small gating head (contextual bandit) toggles optional fragments (CoT, self-check, tool hints) per input to trade accuracy vs cost while respecting safety/schema constraints. Train via logged bandit data with doubly robust off-policy evaluation.

7) Drift adaptation + residual correction (new)
- Version drift: Few-shot recalibration adjusts model-ID embedding offsets and conformal bands.
- kNN residual corrector maps (features → residuals) for last-mile error correction without full retraining.

8) Engineering and integration (concrete code-level changes)
- New modules:
  • fragment_grammar.py: Fragment/PIR classes, CFG/JSON Schema, immutability policies.
  • validator.py: finite-state validator + regex/JSON validators; SAT-like checks for guardrail monotonicity.
  • surrogate.py: Fragment-graph Transformer + deep ensembles; calibration utilities (temp scaling, split/weighted conformal).
  • acquisition.py: robust objectives (mean−α·std/CVaR), paraphrase/model panel generators, DPP/diversification, early-stopping LCBs.
  • router.py: contextual bandit gating with DR-OPE, safety-aware toggle policy.
  • drift_adapter.py: few-shot recalibration, residual kNN corrector.
  • logging.py + FragBench builder: unified logs of (prompt, PIR, input, outputs, metrics, safety flags).
- Minimal API surface:
  • propose(n, task_spec, budget, constraints) -> List[candidates with PIR, predicted LCBs, risks].
  • learn(batch_logs) -> update surrogate, router, residuals.
- Drop-in for OPRO/EvoPrompt: replace their free-form mutation step with propose(); keep their scorers; enable early-stop wrapper. For ZOPO: replace fixed pool with SURGE’s generator; optionally fuse ZOPO’s NTK-GP and SURGE UQ via product-of-experts in acquisition.

9) Training data and benchmarks (FragBench)
- Datasets: GSM8K, BBH, MMLU, CNN/DM summarization, HumanEval, tool-augmented tasks; optional VLM extension with visual fragments.
- Budgets: 100–500 black-box API calls per task slice.
- Metrics: task accuracy/F1; robust accuracy under paraphrase/version shifts; token cost/latency; safety/format violation rates; surrogate ECE; LCB coverage; regret vs robust objective; gains from router/drift adapter; ablations on grammar/UQ/acquisition.

Differences vs prior work (novelty)
- APE/OPRO/Promptbreeder/EvoPrompt: LLM-only search, no fragment IR, no calibrated risk, no distributionally robust acquisition. SURGE adds PIR+validator, calibrated surrogate with conformal LCBs, robust multi-objective selection, and early stopping—cutting API calls and adding guarantees.
- ZOPO: Local optimization over a static pool with NTK-GP. SURGE dynamically generates constraint-safe edits via typed grammar, learns a cross-task/model surrogate with explicit calibration, and optimizes for paraphrase/version robustness.
- BBT/PEZ: Require embedding/gradient access and produce non-auditable prompts. SURGE is fully black-box, fragment-interpretable, and governance-ready; gradients reside only in local surrogates/OSS models.
- PROMPTIST: RL for T2I with proxy rewards; task- and modality-specific. SURGE is task-general for NLP/VLMs, with fragment-level causal attribution and conformal reliability.

Expected impact
- Efficiency and predictability: 5–10× fewer target API calls via surrogate filtering, robust acquisition, and early stopping; budgets are user-controllable.
- Robustness to drift and rephrasing: Optimizing over paraphrase/model panels yields prompts that survive API version changes and input variance, reducing maintenance.
- Reliability and governance: Fragment-level edits + validator guarantee safety/format constraints; conformal LCBs provide measurable reliability suitable for audits/compliance.
- Interpretability and reuse: Typed fragments, saliency maps, and causal A/B logs enable human audit and cross-task reuse; PIR libraries and a cross-model surrogate become reusable priors.
- Practical deployment: A drop-in propose()/learn() for OPRO/EvoPrompt/ZOPO-style systems enabling cost-aware, auditable prompt optimization in enterprise LMaaS, with optional instance-adaptive routing.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Experiments to validate SURGE-Prompt (Surrogate-Guided, Robust, and Modular Prompt Optimization)

Common setup (applies to all experiments)
- Environment: Python 3.10+, PyTorch 2.x, transformers, accelerate, vLLM or llama.cpp (for local OSS LLM), numpy, pandas, scikit-learn, scipy, networkx, captum (attribution), jsonschema, regex, ray/tqdm.
- Target black-box LLM(s): any hosted API (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic). Use a single primary model (A) for optimization and optionally a second/updated model (B) for drift evaluation.
- Proxy OSS model for cheap scoring/edits: e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct or Mistral-7B-Instruct via vLLM.
- Datasets (FragBench slices):
  • Reasoning: GSM8K (1k subset train/dev/test: 200/200/600), BBH (8 tasks × 50 dev + 100 test each)
  • Summarization: CNN/DailyMail (200 dev + 400 test)
  • Classification/format: MMLU (4 subjects × 100 dev + 200 test) with JSON output schema
- Safety/format checkers: jsonschema for outputs; light toxicity/safety classifier (detoxify or Llama-Guard-like OSS) for a proxy safety score; simple regex for PII red flags.
- SURGE modules to implement/use:
  • fragment_grammar.py, validator.py, surrogate.py, acquisition.py, router.py, drift_adapter.py, logging.py
- Minimal SURGE API:
  • propose(n, task_spec, budget, constraints) -> List[candidates with PIR, predicted LCBs, risks]
  • learn(batch_logs) -> update surrogate, router, residuals
- Reproducibility: 3 seeds per setting; report mean ± 95% CI; paired permutation test where applicable.

Experiment 1 — Budgeted effectiveness, efficiency, and reliability vs baselines
- Goal: Show SURGE achieves higher task quality under tight API budgets with calibrated reliability and fewer safety/format violations.
- Hypotheses:
  • H1: SURGE reaches ≥ baseline accuracy with 5–10× fewer target-API calls.
  • H2: Conformal LCBs achieve target coverage (e.g., 90–95%) on pass@task-quality and constraint violations.
  • H3: Early-stopping LCBs reduce wasted evaluations by ≥30% with negligible regret.
- Tasks: GSM8K, 4 BBH tasks, CNN/DailyMail (summary with JSON schema), MMLU subset.
- Target model A: gpt-4o-mini (or analogous). Token/call budget per task slice: 300–500 calls.
- Baselines:
  • OPRO (reimplementation): LLM proposes free-form prompt edits; select by observed score.
  • EvoPrompt/Promptbreeder-style evolutionary loop (free-form mutations).
  • ZOPO-like local search on an initial pool with NTK-GP or cosine-embedding surrogate.
  • Random/beam search over free-form prompts (token-capped).
  • Ablation: SURGE without calibration (no conformal, no UCB/LCB), without validator (soft checks only).
- Metrics:
  • Primary: task performance (accuracy/F1 for reasoning/classification; ROUGE/LF + schema validity for summarization).
  • Cost/efficiency: target-API calls, tokens, latency; calls-to-target-score.
  • Reliability: schema violation rate, safety violation rate; ECE of surrogate success probability; LCB coverage (fraction of times true score ≥ predicted LCB).
  • Search quality: best-of-batch score per round; cumulative regret vs robust objective.
- Procedure:
  1) Seed each task with a baseline prompt and fragmentize to PIR; enforce schema and safety fragments immutable.
  2) Run each optimizer under identical total call budgets and per-round batch size (e.g., 8 candidates/round).
  3) For SURGE: acquisition uses conformal LCBs with early stopping; paraphrase panel disabled here to focus on efficiency (enable in Exp2).
  4) Evaluate candidates on dev split only during optimization; choose final prompt(s) by LCB; freeze and test on held-out test split once.
  5) Log every call and metric; compute CIs and significance vs best baseline.
- Expected results:
  • SURGE meets or exceeds baselines in final test performance while using far fewer API calls and tokens; LCBs well-calibrated (coverage within ±3% of target); fewer schema/safety violations.
- Minimal Python skeleton:

import random, numpy as np
from logging_mod import LogStore
from fragment_grammar import to_pir, FragmentBank
from validator import Validator
from surrogate import Surrogate
from acquisition import select_batch
from target_api import call_llm  # wraps provider API

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
val = Validator()
surr = Surrogate()
logs = LogStore()

# seed prompt -> PIR
seed_prompt = open('seeds/gsm8k.txt').read()
pir = to_pir(seed_prompt)
frag_bank = FragmentBank()

budget_calls = 400
per_round = 8
rounds = budget_calls // per_round

for r in range(rounds):
    candidates = surr.propose(n=per_round, task_spec='gsm8k', pir=pir,
                              constraints={'schema': 'json', 'immutables': ['safety','schema']})
    valid_cands = [c for c in candidates if val.check(c.pir)]
    # early-stopping evaluation on dev set subset
    eval_batch = []
    for c in valid_cands:
        scores = []
        for k, item in enumerate(dev_subset.sample(K:=8, rng=rng)):
            y = call_llm(model='gpt-4o-mini', prompt=c.render(item))
            s = task_metric(item, y)
            scores.append(s)
            # early stop if Wilson LCB below threshold
            if wilson_lcb(sum(scores), len(scores), alpha=0.1) < c.predicted_lcb:
                break
        eval_batch.append({'cand': c, 'scores': scores})
    logs.add(eval_batch)
    surr.learn(eval_batch)

# pick best by LCB on full dev; evaluate once on test
best = pick_best_by_lcb(logs)
results = evaluate_on_test(best, test_set)
print(results)


Experiment 2 — Paraphrase and model-version robustness with conformal guardrails and drift adaptation
- Goal: Demonstrate distributional robustness across paraphrases and model upgrades, and effectiveness of few-shot drift recalibration and instance-adaptive routing.
- Hypotheses:
  • H4: Optimizing mean − α·std (or CVaR) across paraphrases/model-IDs reduces robust drop (worst-k%) vs mean-only selection by ≥30% relative.
  • H5: Few-shot recalibration (≤30 calls) restores LCB coverage on new model to target without large cost.
  • H6: Instance-adaptive router lowers tokens by ≥20% with ≤1% absolute performance loss.
- Setup:
  • Optimize on model A (e.g., gpt-4o-mini-2025-05) Dev set only; select final prompt by robust LCB.
  • Evaluate on: (i) paraphrased inputs, (ii) model B (e.g., gpt-4o-latest or a different provider), (iii) combined stress (paraphrase × model B).
  • Paraphrase panel: 4 paraphrases per input via OSS LLM + simple template rewrites; sanity-check with a small semantic similarity threshold to avoid label drift.
  • Model-ID panel: use learned embeddings for A and two plausible neighbors (A′, B′) as proxies during acquisition.
- Metrics:
  • Robust accuracy: mean, std, and CVaR_10% across paraphrase×model settings; worst-case over the panel.
  • Drift sensitivity: Δ accuracy from A to B; proportion of instances flipping correctness.
  • Reliability: LCB coverage on A before and after drift; on B after recalibration (target 90–95%).
  • Cost: tokens with/without instance-adaptive router.
- Procedure:
  1) Acquisition objective: maximize E[score] − α·Std[score] with α in {0, 0.5, 1.0} or minimize CVaR_10% subject to safety/schema hard constraints.
  2) During propose(), generate 3–5 paraphrases per input for cheap proxy scoring with OSS model; estimate robust stats with the surrogate.
  3) After selecting top candidates, call model A on dev to confirm; fit split conformal bounds and calibrate temperature.
  4) Freeze best prompt and evaluate on test under: (A, original), (A, paraphrase), (B, original), (B, paraphrase).
  5) Drift adaptation: collect 30 labeled calls on model B dev; run drift_adapter.recalibrate() and kNN residual corrector; re-evaluate coverage and robust metrics.
  6) Router: enable per-instance toggle of CoT/self-check fragment using DR-OPE; measure token savings and accuracy change.
- Baselines/ablations:
  • SURGE-mean (α=0), SURGE-CVaR, SURGE-no-panel (no paraphrases, no model-ID panel), SURGE-no-recalib, SURGE-no-router.
  • OPRO/EvoPrompt optimized on A; evaluate their prompts on B and paraphrases.
- Minimal Python skeleton:

from acquisition import robust_select_batch
from paraphrase import generate_panel
from drift_adapter import recalibrate, residual_knn

alpha = 0.5
for r in range(rounds):
    cand_pool = surr.propose(n=per_round, task_spec=task, pir=pir, constraints=constraints)
    for c in cand_pool:
        para_items = generate_panel(dev_subset, m=4)
        c.robust_stats = surr.predict_panel(c, para_items, model_id_panel=['A','A_prime','B_prime'])
    batch = robust_select_batch(cand_pool, objective='mean-std', alpha=alpha, diversify=True)
    eval_and_update(batch)

# Drift recalibration
calib_logs = collect_calls(model='B', data=dev_calib[:30], prompt=best)
surr.temperature, conf_split = recalibrate(calib_logs)
residual_knn.fit(features(calib_logs), residuals(calib_logs))

# Router evaluation
router.enable(True)
router_results = evaluate_with_router(best, test_set, model='B')
router.disable()


Experiment 3 — Surrogate calibration, saliency-guided edits, and causal interpretability
- Goal: Validate the uncertainty-aware surrogate and the fragment-level saliency/causal edit machinery, and quantify benefits of the PIR+validator.
- Hypotheses:
  • H7: Calibrated surrogate achieves lower ECE and higher pairwise ranking AUC vs baselines (uncalibrated LLM scoring, embedding GP).
  • H8: Saliency-guided, constrained span edits outperform random/heuristic edits in sample efficiency (queries to reach top-decile prompt).
  • H9: PIR+validator reduces schema/safety violations by ≥50% vs free-form mutation while maintaining or improving quality.
- Data: Use GSM8K and MMLU dev-only for training the surrogate; hold out small test slices for surrogate evaluation (no prompt re-optimization during measurement).
- Baselines/ablations:
  • Flat-string surrogate (no fragments), No-calibration, No-ensembles, Random-edit, No-attribution, No-DPP diversification, No-validator.
- Metrics:
  • Surrogate: ECE, Brier score, pairwise ranking AUC, Kendall-τ between predicted and observed candidate ranks.
  • Search efficiency: API calls to reach target score; area-under-improvement-curve.
  • Interpretability/causality: fraction of high-saliency spans with positive causal A/B effect; average treatment effect per fragment type.
  • Safety/format: violation rates under mutation.
- Procedure:
  1) Build an initial candidate set via random fragment edits (validator-on) and log outcomes on a small dev set; train surrogate ensembles.
  2) Compute integrated gradients or rollout attributions over fragments/spans; pick top-k locations; perform mask-and-infill with OSS LLM respecting fragment types and constraints.
  3) Run micro-batch A/B tests toggling single fragments to estimate causal deltas; update surrogate online with these residuals.
  4) Compare to random edits and flat-string edits under the same budget; track sample efficiency and violations.
  5) Evaluate surrogate calibration on a held-out candidate set; report ECE and ranking AUC.
- Minimal Python skeleton:

from captum.attr import IntegratedGradients
from edit_ops import mask_and_infill

# Train surrogate from random edits
cands0 = random_fragment_edits(pir, n=200, validator=val)
labels0 = eval_candidates(cands0, dev_small)
surr.fit(cands0, labels0)

# Saliency
ig = IntegratedGradients(surr.forward_for_attr)
attr = ig.attribute(inputs=pir.to_tensor(), target='success_prob')
hot_spans = select_top_spans(attr, top_p=0.2)

# Constrained edits via mask+infill
edited = []
for span in hot_spans:
    e = mask_and_infill(pir, span, oss_llm='llama-3-8b', constraints=val)
    if val.check(e):
        edited.append(e)

# Micro-batch A/B causal checks
ab_pairs = []
for e in edited:
    y_a = batch_eval(e, dev_micro)
    y_b = batch_eval(toggle_fragment(e), dev_micro)
    ab_pairs.append((e, y_a, y_b))
    surr.update_with_ab(e, y_a, y_b)

# Compare to random edits
random_improve_curve = search_loop_random(pir)
saliency_improve_curve = search_loop_saliency(pir)
compare_curves(random_improve_curve, saliency_improve_curve)


Evaluation and analysis notes (all experiments)
- Confidence/coverage: Use split-conformal prediction on dev; report empirical coverage on test. For binomial outcomes, compute Wilson intervals.
- Statistical tests: Paired permutation or Wilcoxon signed-rank across items; report effect sizes.
- Failure and diagnostics: If ECE > target, increase ensemble size or recalibration temperature; if violation rate exceeds cap, harden validator and penalize in acquisition; if drift is large, increase paraphrase/model panel diversity.
- Resource estimate: Each task slice completes within 6–12 GPU hours for proxy models (7–8B) and ≤1–2k black-box calls across all repeats; fits in a single 24GB GPU or CPU for validator/calibration.
- Deliverables: Plots of score vs calls, robust accuracy under drift, ECE/coverage histograms, violation rates, and ablation bars; export audit logs (prompt fragments, edits, risks) for governance review.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
SURGE-Prompt Experimental Plan and Detailed Protocols

Common setup (applies to all experiments)
- Environment and packages
  • Python 3.10+, PyTorch 2.2+, transformers>=4.41, accelerate, vllm or llama-cpp-python (local OSS LLM), numpy, pandas, scikit-learn, scipy, networkx, captum, jsonschema, regex, sentence-transformers, detoxify, textstat (readability), rouge-score or evaluate, ray, tqdm.
  • Optional: peft and bitsandbytes if you want QLoRA/4-bit loading for OSS models, faiss-cpu for fast kNN.
- Hardware
  • Single GPU 24 GB VRAM sufficient for 7–8B OSS inference and surrogate training; CPU works for validator and calibration.
- Target black-box LLM(s)
  • Model A (primary for optimization): e.g., gpt-4o-mini-2025-05 (abstracted via a target_api.call())
  • Model B (for drift): e.g., gpt-4o-latest (or an analogous updated version/provider)
- Proxy OSS LLM for cheap scoring/edits
  • Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct or Mistral-7B-Instruct via vLLM; decoding: temperature=0.6, top_p=0.9, max_new_tokens=256
- Datasets (FragBench slices) and splits (3 seeds; shuffle with seed)
  • GSM8K: 1k subset split 200/200/600 (train/dev/test)
  • BBH: pick 8 tasks × (50 dev + 100 test each)
  • CNN/DailyMail: 200 dev + 400 test
  • MMLU: select 4 subjects × (100 dev + 200 test each); use JSON output schema for multiple choice
- Safety/format checkers
  • jsonschema for structured outputs; Detoxify (unitary/toxic-bert) for toxicity; regex for PII (email, phone, SSN-like patterns)
- SURGE modules to implement/use
  • fragment_grammar.py: Prompt IR (PIR) fragment types and serialization
  • validator.py: CFG/JSON Schema/regex checks; immutable fragment policies
  • surrogate.py: fragment-graph transformer with deep ensemble; temperature scaling; split conformal bounds
  • acquisition.py: robust objectives (mean−α·std, CVaR), paraphrase/model panel handling, DPP diversification, early-stopping LCBs
  • router.py: contextual bandit gating for optional fragments (CoT/self-check/tool hints)
  • drift_adapter.py: few-shot recalibration and residual kNN corrector
  • logging.py: unified logs with auditability
- Minimal SURGE API
  • propose(n, task_spec, budget, constraints) -> list of candidates with PIR, predicted LCBs, risks
  • learn(batch_logs) -> update surrogate, router, residuals
- Reproducibility
  • 3 seeds per setting; report mean ± 95% CI; paired permutation or Wilcoxon signed-rank where applicable
  • Cache paraphrases and API responses; write deterministic pre-processing; fix torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic=True

Concrete implementation details
1) Prompt IR (PIR) and validator
- Fragment types
  • reasoning_trigger, step_plan, tool_verifier_hints, output_schema, tone_style, safety_guardrails, brevity_cost_policy
- Fragment metadata
  • fields: id, type, content, max_tokens, reserved_tokens, immutable:bool, markers (e.g., JSON schema reference), version, provenance
- Example PIR JSON
  {
    "fragments": [
      {"id": "f1", "type": "safety_guardrails", "content": "Follow the safety rules ...", "immutable": true},
      {"id": "f2", "type": "output_schema", "content": "You must return JSON conforming to ...", "immutable": true},
      {"id": "f3", "type": "reasoning_trigger", "content": "Think step by step and verify each step.", "immutable": false},
      {"id": "f4", "type": "step_plan", "content": "1) Parse the question ... 2) Compute ... 3) Check ...", "immutable": false},
      {"id": "f5", "type": "brevity_cost_policy", "content": "Keep answers concise. Avoid unnecessary verbosity.", "immutable": false}
    ]
  }
- Validator rules
  • Finite-state checks for order constraints (safety and schema must precede reasoning fragments)
  • JSON Schema validation for output_schema
  • Regex for reserved markers (e.g., "BEGIN JSON"/"END JSON")
  • Max length per fragment and total max; immutability enforced for safety/schema
  • Guardrail monotonicity: edits cannot remove mandatory constraints; SAT-like checks via simple rule engine

2) Surrogate model
- Inputs per candidate
  • Fragment graph: sequence with type embeddings + position; adjacency via segment markers
  • Task descriptor embedding (task id, few meta tags)
  • Prompt stats (length, num lists, lexical diversity) normalized
  • Cheap proxy scores from OSS LLM on small micro-batch (optional)
  • Model-ID embedding (family/version/decoding)
  • Optional input-difficulty features (e.g., textstat, length, entropy)
- Architecture
  • Tokenization-by-fragment: each fragment content is encoded by a frozen sentence embedding model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) to 384d; concatenated with learned type embedding (8–16d) and scalar features
  • TransformerEncoder (2–4 layers, d_model=512, nhead=8); pooled CLS
  • Deep ensemble of 5 independent models with different seeds; MC-dropout 0.1 for additional UQ
  • Multi-head outputs: p_success (sigmoid), p_format_violation, p_safety_violation, cost_pred (regression)
  • Pairwise ranking head: learns ordering between candidate pairs using margin ranking loss
- Training losses
  • L = BCE(success) + 0.5*BCE(format) + 0.5*BCE(safety) + 0.2*MSE(cost) + 0.2*RankLoss
  • Class imbalance handled with focal-BCE(gamma=1.5) when needed
- Calibration
  • Temperature scaling on dev fold to minimize NLL
  • Split conformal prediction to produce per-candidate LCBs for success and UCBs for violations

3) Acquisition
- Objectives
  • Base: maximize conformal LCB on success subject to P(format_violation) ≤ τ_f and P(safety_violation) ≤ τ_s
  • Robust: maximize mean − α·std across paraphrase × model-id panel or minimize CVaR_10%
- Diversity
  • DPP based on cosine similarity over fragment-level embeddings; kernel = E E^T + 0.01 I
- Early stopping
  • Wilson score lower bound computed online during evaluation on a dev micro-batch; stop candidates whose Wilson LCB falls below their predicted LCB or a dynamic cutoff

4) Instance-adaptive router
- Fragments eligible for toggling: reasoning_trigger (CoT), self-check instructions, tool hints
- Contextual bandit policy with linear features; action = {toggle on/off set of fragments}; reward = score − λ·tokens
- Train with logged data using doubly robust off-policy estimation (propensity from epsilon-greedy logging)

5) Drift adaptation
- Few-shot recalibration (≤30 calls on model B dev)
  • Refit temperature; update conformal quantiles on split dev; adjust model-id embedding offset by small linear probe
- Residual corrector
  • kNN regressor over features -> residuals; add to surrogate mean prediction at inference for last-mile correction

6) Metrics and computation
- Task metrics
  • GSM8K/BBH: exact correctness; few-shot self-check allowed per PIR
  • MMLU: multiple-choice accuracy; enforce JSON schema, treat invalid JSON as incorrect and schema-violation event
  • CNN/DM: ROUGE-L F; also compute schema validity for a minimal JSON wrapper (e.g., {"summary": str, "evidence": [str]})
- Reliability metrics
  • ECE for success probability (10 bins); Brier score; LCB coverage (fraction of times true score ≥ predicted LCB)
  • Safety/format violation rates
- Efficiency metrics
  • Number of target API calls; total tokens; latency; area-under-improvement-curve (AUC over calls)

7) Minimal helper code snippets
- Wilson LCB
  def wilson_lcb(successes, n, alpha=0.1):
      if n == 0: return 0.0
      from math import sqrt
      import scipy.stats as st
      z = st.norm.ppf(1 - alpha/2)
      phat = successes / n
      denom = 1 + z*z/n
      center = phat + z*z/(2*n)
      radius = z*sqrt((phat*(1-phat) + z*z/(4*n))/n)
      return (center - radius) / denom
- ECE
  def expected_calibration_error(probs, labels, n_bins=10):
      import numpy as np
      bins = np.linspace(0,1,n_bins+1)
      ece = 0.0
      for i in range(n_bins):
          idx = (probs > bins[i]) & (probs <= bins[i+1])
          if idx.sum() == 0: continue
          conf = probs[idx].mean()
          acc = labels[idx].mean()
          ece += (idx.mean()) * abs(acc - conf)
      return ece
- DPP selection (greedy)
  def dpp_greedy(embs, k):
      import numpy as np
      K = embs @ embs.T + 1e-2*np.eye(len(embs))
      selected = []
      C = np.zeros_like(K)
      for _ in range(min(k, len(embs))):
          gains = np.diag(K - C)
          i = int(np.argmax(gains))
          selected.append(i)
          ki = K[:, i:i+1]
          denom = max(K[i, i] - C[i, i], 1e-6)
          C += (ki @ ki.T) / denom
      return selected


Experiment 1 — Budgeted effectiveness, efficiency, and reliability vs baselines
- Goals and hypotheses
  • H1: SURGE meets or exceeds baselines using 5–10× fewer target-API calls
  • H2: Conformal LCBs achieve target coverage (90–95%) for success and constraints
  • H3: Early-stopping LCBs cut wasted evaluations by ≥30% with negligible regret
- Tasks and budgets
  • GSM8K (200 dev, 600 test), 4 BBH tasks, CNN/DM, MMLU subset
  • Target model A; per-task budget 300–500 calls; batch size per round 8; rounds = budget//8
- Baselines
  • OPRO-like: black-box LLM proposes free-form edits; selection by observed dev score
  • EvoPrompt/Promptbreeder-style: free-form evolutionary mutations + selection
  • ZOPO-like: local search over initial pool; NTK-GP or cosine GP over embeddings
  • Random/beam search over free-form prompts
  • Ablations: SURGE without calibration (no conformal/LCB), SURGE without validator
- Non-overlap design
  • Paraphrase/model panels disabled in Exp1 to isolate budgeted efficiency and calibration; those are enabled in Exp2
- Procedure
  1) Initialize seed prompts per task; convert to PIR; safety_guardrails and output_schema immutable
  2) For each optimizer, run identical number of rounds and batch size under same token budget caps
  3) SURGE acquisition: prioritize candidates by conformal LCB; enable early stopping with Wilson LCB on mini dev micro-batches (e.g., 8–16 items)
  4) Learning loop: after each round, update surrogate with batch logs (including proxy features and observed labels)
  5) Select final prompt by highest LCB on full dev; evaluate once on held-out test
  6) Log cost tokens, latency, safety/format violations, ECE, LCB coverage
- Key hyperparameters
  • Ensemble size: 5; Transformer layers: 3; d_model: 512; dropout: 0.1
  • Conformal alpha: 0.1; temperature scaling via 100-example dev calibration
  • Early-stop threshold: drop candidate if Wilson LCB < candidate predicted LCB or below 0.2 absolute after at least 6 items
  • Max fragment length per type: safety/schema ≤ 150 tokens; others ≤ 250 tokens
- Metrics
  • Primary: GSM8K/BBH/MMLU accuracy; CNN/DM ROUGE-L F with schema-validity rate (invalid JSON => score 0 for that item and count violation)
  • Efficiency: target-API calls, tokens, time; AUC over calls
  • Reliability: ECE, LCB coverage for success; schema/safety violation rates; regret vs robust objective (mean-only in Exp1)
- Example code skeleton (end-to-end loop)
  import numpy as np, random, time
  from fragment_grammar import to_pir, FragmentBank
  from validator import Validator
  from surrogate import Surrogate
  from acquisition import select_batch
  from logging_mod import LogStore
  from target_api import call_llm
  from metrics import task_metric, schema_ok

  rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
  seeds = [0,1,2]

  def run_surged(seed, task_spec, dev_data, test_data, budget_calls=400, per_round=8):
      np.random.seed(seed); random.seed(seed)
      val = Validator()
      surr = Surrogate(seed=seed)
      logs = LogStore(task_spec)
      seed_prompt = open(f'seeds/{task_spec}.txt').read()
      pir0 = to_pir(seed_prompt)
      pir = val.repair(pir0)  # ensure canonical order
      rounds = budget_calls // per_round

      for r in range(rounds):
          cand_pool = surr.propose(n=per_round*3, task_spec=task_spec, pir=pir,
                                   constraints={'immutables': ['safety_guardrails','output_schema']})
          cand_pool = [c for c in cand_pool if val.check(c.pir)]
          batch = select_batch(cand_pool, k=per_round, diversify=True)

          eval_batch = []
          for c in batch:
              successes, n_eval, scores = 0, 0, []
              for item in rng.choice(dev_data, size=12, replace=False):
                  prompt_txt = c.render(item)
                  y = call_llm(model='A', prompt=prompt_txt)
                  s = task_metric(task_spec, item, y)
                  scores.append(s)
                  successes += int(s > 0.5)
                  n_eval += 1
                  if wilson_lcb(successes, n_eval, alpha=0.1) < c.pred_lcb:
                      break
              eval_batch.append({'cand': c.to_dict(), 'scores': scores, 'n_eval': n_eval})
          logs.add(eval_batch)
          surr.learn(eval_batch)

      best = logs.best_by_lcb(surr)
      test_scores = []
      for item in test_data:
          y = call_llm(model='A', prompt=best['cand'].render(item))
          test_scores.append(task_metric(task_spec, item, y))
      return np.mean(test_scores), logs

  # Baselines (OPRO/EvoPrompt/ZOPO) follow similar API with different propose()

- Reliability enhancements included
  • Pre-call validator eliminates unsafe/invalid prompts
  • Early stopping reduces waste while respecting predicted LCBs
  • Conformal LCBs provide audited coverage estimates
- Expected outcomes
  • SURGE achieves comparable or higher test performance with substantially fewer calls (≥5× reduction) and lower violation rates; ECE < 0.07; coverage within ±3% of target


Experiment 2 — Paraphrase and model-version robustness with conformal guardrails and drift adaptation
- Goals and hypotheses
  • H4: Robust objective (mean − α·std or CVaR) reduces robust drop by ≥30% vs mean-only
  • H5: ≤30-call recalibration restores LCB coverage on model B to target
  • H6: Router reduces tokens by ≥20% with ≤1% accuracy loss
- Setup
  • Optimize on model A using robust acquisition over paraphrase × model-id panel
  • Evaluate robustness on (A, original), (A, paraphrase), (B, original), (B, paraphrase)
  • Paraphrase panel: 4 paraphrases per input from OSS LLM; accept only if semantic similarity ≥ 0.85 (cosine over sentence-transformers embeddings)
  • Model-id panel: embeddings for A, A′, B′ (learned vectors); acquisition predicts over panel
- Procedure
  1) During propose(), for each candidate compute surrogate predictions across m paraphrases and model-id panel; objective = mean − α·std (α∈{0,0.5,1.0}) or CVaR_10%
  2) Select diverse batch via DPP; evaluate on model A dev; update surrogate and conformal bounds
  3) Freeze best prompt by robust LCB; evaluate on test under four settings above
  4) Drift adaptation: sample 30 dev items on model B; run recalibration (temperature + split conformal) and fit residual kNN; re-evaluate coverage/robust metrics
  5) Instance-adaptive router: enable gating for CoT/self-check; train via DR-OPE on logged bandit data; measure token savings and accuracy delta
- Metrics
  • Robust accuracy: mean, std, worst-10% (CVaR_10%) across settings; robust drop = mean − worst-case
  • Drift sensitivity: Δ accuracy from A to B; flip rate
  • Reliability: LCB coverage before/after recalibration
  • Cost: tokens with/without router; latency impact
- Baselines/ablations
  • SURGE-mean (α=0), SURGE-CVaR, SURGE-no-panel, SURGE-no-recalib, SURGE-no-router
  • OPRO/EvoPrompt optimized on A; evaluated on B and paraphrases
- Key hyperparameters
  • α=0.5 default; m=4 paraphrases; model-id panel size 3; DPP kernel jitter=1e-2
  • Router λ (token penalty) tuned on dev via grid {0.0, 1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3 tokens^-1}
- Example paraphrase and robustness code
  from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
  from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
  import torch, numpy as np

  st_model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

  def paraphrase_batch_texts(texts, oss_pipe, max_attempts=3):
      paras = []
      for t in texts:
          best = []
          for _ in range(max_attempts):
              p = oss_pipe(f"Paraphrase while preserving meaning: {t}\nParaphrase:",
                           max_new_tokens=180, temperature=0.8, top_p=0.9)
              best.append(p)
          # select 4 unique paraphrases with sim≥0.85
          emb = st_model.encode([t]+best, convert_to_tensor=True)
          sims = util.cos_sim(emb[0:1], emb[1:]).cpu().numpy()[0]
          picks = [best[i] for i in np.argsort(-sims)[:4] if sims[i] >= 0.85]
          paras.append(picks)
      return paras

  def robust_stats_from_surrogate(cand, items, model_ids):
      scores = []
      for mid in model_ids:
          for it in items:
              s = surrogate.predict(cand, item=it, model_id=mid)
              scores.append(s)
      arr = np.array(scores)
      mean = arr.mean(); std = arr.std();
      cvar = np.mean(np.partition(arr, int(0.1*len(arr)))[:int(0.1*len(arr))])
      return {'mean': mean, 'std': std, 'cvar10': cvar}

- Drift recalibration code sketch
  from drift_adapter import recalibrate_temperature, split_conformal_bounds, ResidualKNN

  calib_logs = collect_calls(model='B', data=dev_data[:30], prompt=best_prompt)
  T = recalibrate_temperature(calib_logs)  # optimize NLL over logits
  conf = split_conformal_bounds(calib_logs, alpha=0.1)
  rk = ResidualKNN(k=15).fit(calib_logs)
  surrogate.set_temperature(T)
  surrogate.set_conformal(conf)
  surrogate.set_residual_corrector(rk)

- Router training/evaluation sketch
  from router import BanditRouter
  router = BanditRouter(actions=["cot_on","cot_off"], seed=0)
  router.fit_with_dro(logged_data=logs.router_data())
  enable_router(True)
  results_on = evaluate_with_router(best_prompt, test_data, model='B')
  enable_router(False)
  
- Expected outcomes
  • Robust objective improves worst-case (paraphrase×model) accuracy and reduces robust drop by ≥30% vs mean-only
  • Few-shot recalibration restores coverage near target with negligible extra cost
  • Router achieves ≥20% token savings with ≤1% absolute performance loss; optional small latency gain


Experiment 3 — Surrogate calibration, saliency-guided edits, and causal interpretability
- Goals and hypotheses
  • H7: Calibrated surrogate beats baselines on ECE and ranking AUC
  • H8: Saliency-guided constrained edits reach top-decile prompts with fewer queries than random/heuristic edits
  • H9: PIR+validator cuts schema/safety violations by ≥50% vs free-form mutation without hurting quality
- Data
  • GSM8K and MMLU dev-only for surrogate training; small held-out candidate sets for evaluation (no prompt re-optimization during measurement)
- Baselines/ablations
  • Flat-string surrogate (no fragments), No-calibration, No-ensembles, Random-edit, No-attribution, No-DPP, No-validator
- Procedure
  1) Pretrain surrogate with 200–500 random fragment edits (validator-on). Evaluate candidates on small dev to collect labels. Fit deep ensemble.
  2) Compute attribution over fragment/spans using Captum Integrated Gradients on surrogate’s fragment encoder. Select top 20% spans per candidate.
  3) Mask-and-infill constrained edits using OSS LLM conditioned on fragment type; accept only validator-passing edits.
  4) Micro-batch A/B causal tests: toggle single fragments (e.g., step_plan variants) and estimate causal delta; update surrogate with residuals (online fine-tuning with small lr).
  5) Compare to random edits and flat-string edits under identical budgets; track calls to reach target score percentile and AUC over improvement curve.
  6) Evaluate surrogate calibration on held-out candidate set: ECE, Brier, pairwise ranking AUC, Kendall-τ.
  7) Safety/format: report violation rates under mutation for PIR+validator vs free-form.
- Key hyperparameters
  • Attributions: m_steps=64 IG; top_p=0.2 spans; edit budget per round: 16
  • OSS infill decoding: temperature=0.7, top_p=0.9, repetition_penalty=1.05
  • Online learning: lr=5e-5, 1–2 epochs per round on new data, early stop on dev NLL
- Example code snippets
  from captum.attr import IntegratedGradients

  # Train ensemble surrogate
  cands, labels = bootstrap_random_edits(pir, n=400, validator=Validator())
  surr = Surrogate(ensemble_size=5)
  surr.fit(cands, labels)

  # Saliency over fragments
  ig = IntegratedGradients(surr.forward_for_attr)  # returns logit for success
  inputs = pir.to_tensor()  # fragment embeddings + features
  attr = ig.attribute(inputs, target=0, n_steps=64)
  hot_spans = select_top_spans(attr, top_p=0.2)

  # Constrained mask-and-infill
  from edit_ops import mask_and_infill
  edited_cands = []
  for span in hot_spans:
      e = mask_and_infill(pir, span, oss_llm='llama-3-8b', condition={'type': span.type})
      if Validator().check(e):
          edited_cands.append(e)

  # Micro-batch A/B causal checks
  def ab_effect(c1, c2, data_micro):
      s1 = batch_eval(c1, data_micro); s2 = batch_eval(c2, data_micro)
      return np.mean(s2) - np.mean(s1)
  ab_deltas = []
  for e in edited_cands:
      e_toggle = toggle_single_fragment(e)
      ab = ab_effect(e, e_toggle, dev_micro)
      ab_deltas.append(ab)
      surr.update_with_causal(e, ab)

  # Surrogate evaluation
  heldout = generate_heldout_candidates(pir, n=200)
  probs = surr.predict_proba(heldout)
  labels = eval_candidates(heldout, dev_holdout)
  ece = expected_calibration_error(probs, labels)

- Expected outcomes
  • Calibrated ensemble achieves ECE < 0.06, Brier improved over baselines; ranking AUC ≥ 0.8; Kendall-τ significantly higher
  • Saliency-guided edits reach top-decile prompts with 30–50% fewer API calls than random/heuristic edits
  • PIR+validator halves violation rates compared to free-form mutation with equal or better task scores


Module sketch implementations (selected)
- fragment_grammar.py
  import json, copy
  class PIR:
      def __init__(self, fragments): self.fragments = fragments
      def to_json(self): return json.dumps({'fragments': self.fragments}, ensure_ascii=False)
      def render(self, item):
          parts = []
          for f in self.fragments:
              parts.append(f['content'])
          parts.append(f"Input:\n{item['input']}")
          return "\n\n".join(parts)
      def to_tensor(self):
          # produce concatenated fragment embeddings + features (placeholder)
          pass
  def to_pir(raw_prompt):
      # naive segmentation using heuristics + types; in practice use an OSS LLM to segment
      return PIR([
        {'id':'s1','type':'safety_guardrails','content':'Follow the safety rules...', 'immutable':True},
        {'id':'s2','type':'output_schema','content':'Return JSON with fields ...', 'immutable':True},
        {'id':'r1','type':'reasoning_trigger','content':'Think step by step.', 'immutable':False},
      ])
  class FragmentBank:
      def __init__(self): self.store = []
      def add(self, frag): self.store.append(frag)
      def sample(self, frag_type, k=5):
          return [f for f in self.store if f['type']==frag_type][:k]

- validator.py
  from jsonschema import Draft7Validator
  import regex as re
  class Validator:
      def __init__(self, schema=None): self.schema = schema or {"type":"object","properties":{"answer":{"type":"string"}}}
      def check(self, pir):
          # order constraints
          types = [f['type'] for f in pir.fragments]
          if 'output_schema' in types and types.index('output_schema') > types.index('reasoning_trigger'):
              return False
          # immutability check placeholder
          for f in pir.fragments:
              if f.get('immutable') and 'EDITED' in f['content']: return False
          return True
      def repair(self, pir):
          # ensure safety and schema at top
          frags = sorted(pir.fragments, key=lambda f: {'safety_guardrails':0,'output_schema':1}.get(f['type'],2))
          return PIR(frags)

- surrogate.py (high level)
  import torch, torch.nn as nn
  class FragEncoder(nn.Module):
      def __init__(self, d_in=384+16+8, d_model=512, layers=3, heads=8):
          super().__init__()
          enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(d_model, heads, dim_feedforward=1024, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True)
          self.proj = nn.Linear(d_in, d_model)
          self.encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=layers)
          self.head = nn.Linear(d_model, 4)  # success, format, safety, cost
      def forward(self, x):
          h = self.encoder(self.proj(x))
          pooled = h[:,0]  # CLS
          out = self.head(pooled)
          return out
  class Surrogate:
      def __init__(self, ensemble_size=5, seed=0):
          self.models = [FragEncoder() for _ in range(ensemble_size)]
          self.T = 1.0
          self.conf = {'q_low':0.1}
      def fit(self, cands, labels): pass
      def learn(self, eval_batch): pass
      def predict(self, cand, item=None, model_id='A'):
          # returns mean prob and uncertainty; placeholder
          import numpy as np
          p = 0.6; u = 0.1
          return p, u
      def propose(self, n, task_spec, pir, constraints):
          # generate constrained edits using OSS LLM; return list of Candidate objects with pred_lcb
          pass
      def set_temperature(self, T): self.T = T
      def set_conformal(self, conf): self.conf = conf

- acquisition.py
  def select_batch(cand_pool, k=8, diversify=True):
      if not diversify: return sorted(cand_pool, key=lambda c: -c.pred_lcb)[:k]
      embs = np.stack([c.embedding for c in cand_pool])
      idxs = dpp_greedy(embs, k)
      return [cand_pool[i] for i in idxs]


Evaluation and analysis notes (applies to all experiments)
- Confidence/coverage
  • Use split-conformal on dev; report empirical coverage on test and Wilson 95% CI
- Statistical tests
  • Paired permutation test for accuracy deltas across items; Wilcoxon on paired scores; report effect size (Cliff’s delta)
- Diagnostics
  • If ECE high, increase ensemble size or add temperature scaling; consider isotonic regression post-calibration
  • If violation rate exceeds cap, harden validator and add penalty in acquisition score: score = LCB − β·(p_format+p_safety)
  • If drift large, enlarge paraphrase/model-id panel; update residual corrector
- Resource estimates
  • 6–12 GPU hours per task slice for proxy models; ≤2k total black-box calls across repeats
- Deliverables
  • Plots: score vs calls, robust accuracy vs settings, ECE and LCB coverage histograms, violation rates, ablation bars
  • Audit logs: PIRs, fragment edits, risks, conformal thresholds; CSV/Parquet + JSON configs

Integration to avoid redundancy across experiments
- Build and cache a shared FragmentBank and initial surrogate pretraining from Exp3; reuse in Exp1/Exp2 to reduce cold-start noise
- Use the same validator and safety/json schemas across all experiments; only acquisition objectives differ
- Logging and calibration utilities are common; Exp2 adds paraphrase/model panels and drift adaptor; Exp3 focuses on attribution and causal checks

Practical tips and risk controls
- Respect token budgets by tracking per-candidate early stop; abort full-batch if budget risks being exceeded
- Avoid label drift in paraphrases by enforcing similarity thresholds and manual spot checks on a small sample
- Treat any schema parsing failure as both violation and zero score to incentivize robust adherence
- Keep safety_guardrails and output_schema immutable to preserve governance, unless running explicit ablations

Expected global impact across experiments
- Efficiency: 5–10× fewer API calls to reach parity or better than LLM-only search
- Robustness: improved worst-case under paraphrases/drift; stable performance after model updates with few-shot recalibration
- Reliability: measured LCB coverage near target; lower schema/safety violation rates; auditable edit logs and PIRs
experimental_design.experiment_code:
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
SURGE-Prompt: Surrogate-Guided, Robust, and Modular Prompt Optimization (Reproducible Experiments)

Notes and scope
- This is a self-contained, lightweight simulation of the SURGE-Prompt method suitable for functional testing
  and ablation-style comparisons under a controlled synthetic black-box. No external APIs are called.
- All learned components use PyTorch. Heavy models (LLMs, sentence-transformers) are replaced by light surrogates.
- The code prints progress and key metrics, saves all figures as .pdf with names compliant to the requested scheme,
  and runs quickly for testing. You can scale budgets to stress-test.

How to run
- python surge_prompt_experiments.py  # will run Exp1–3 with small budgets and save figures
- The test_quick_run() function runs an even smaller sanity check.

Required Python libraries (install via pip if missing)
- Python>=3.10
- torch (PyTorch>=2.0)
- numpy
- pandas
- scipy
- matplotlib
- seaborn
- tqdm
- regex
- jsonschema

Optional (accelerates or adds features; code has fallbacks and does not strictly require these)
- scikit-learn (for confusion matrix plotting convenience; fallback provided if absent)

All plots saved as .pdf using plt.savefig(..., bbox_inches='tight').
"""

import os
import json
import math
import time
import copy
import random
import string
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Dict, Any, Tuple, Optional

import numpy as np
